stories.headline: Update: Federal Reserve Holds Rates Steady; Powell Announces He Will Remain as Governor After Chair Term Ends
stories.summary:
The Federal Reserve held its benchmark interest rate steady Wednesday amid ongoing uncertainty over the U.S.-Iran conflict and its effects on global energy markets and inflation. Fed Chair Jerome Powell announced he will remain on the Fed's board of governors for an undetermined period after his chairmanship ends May 15, a move that drew sharp criticism from Trump administration allies who viewed it as an attempt to constrain incoming Chair Kevin Warsh.

Powell's decision to stay on the board was described by Fed watchers as a deliberate effort to preserve institutional independence during what he called ongoing legal threats to the central bank. A group of current Fed policymakers also sent a public signal to Warsh that they oppose premature interest rate cuts, suggesting the incoming chair will face significant internal resistance if he moves to rapidly lower rates as Trump has demanded.

Trump allies and administration figures pushed back forcefully, accusing Powell of overstepping and attempting to undermine the president's economic agenda. Supporters of the Fed's current stance argue that with inflation rising to its highest level in nearly two years, holding rates steady is the prudent course, and that Warsh will need to earn consensus rather than impose change unilaterally.

The Fed's meeting was described by analysts as among the most internally divisive in decades. Because rate cuts can spur inflation — already elevated partly due to energy price spikes tied to the Iran conflict — most economists now predict the Fed will hold off on reductions until late 2026 or 2027, leaving Warsh with limited room to maneuver from day one.

stories.headline: Trump Withdraws Casey Means Surgeon General Nomination, Names Fox News Contributor Dr. Nicole Saphier
stories.summary:
President Donald Trump on Thursday pulled the nomination of wellness entrepreneur Dr. Casey Means for surgeon general and named Dr. Nicole Saphier, a radiologist and former Fox News contributor, as her replacement. Means's path forward had stalled in the Senate over questions about her medical credentials and her stance on vaccines.

Saphier is a board-certified radiologist and author who became a prominent television medical commentator during the COVID-19 pandemic. Her nomination is expected to face less resistance in the Senate than Means, though her confirmation timeline remains unclear. Trump also publicly criticized Sen. Bill Cassidy, calling him "very disloyal" for opposing the Means nomination.

The move reflects ongoing turbulence in Trump's health policy appointments, coming as the administration continues to reshape federal health agencies. Means, who had ties to Health Secretary Robert F. Kennedy Jr.'s wellness-focused orbit, had drawn skepticism from both Republican and Democratic senators over her lack of clinical experience.

The surgeon general serves as the nation's top public health official and spokesperson on health matters. Saphier's nomination must still be confirmed by the Senate.

stories.headline: Five Killed in Small Plane Crash Near Austin, Texas
stories.summary:
A small plane crashed in the Wimberley area of Hays County, Texas, on Thursday, killing all five people aboard, according to local authorities. Witnesses reported hearing a loud boom at the time of the crash.

The crash occurred in the Hill Country region southwest of Austin. Details about the aircraft type, the identities of those aboard, and the cause of the crash have not yet been released. Federal aviation investigators are expected to respond to the scene.

Hays County encompasses a growing suburban and rural corridor near Austin and includes the small city of Wimberley, a tourist destination known for its rivers and arts community. The National Transportation Safety Board typically leads investigations into fatal general aviation accidents.

stories.headline: Two Bank Employees Shot and Killed in Kentucky Robbery; Person of Interest in Custody
stories.summary:
Two employees of a U.S. Bank branch in Berea, Kentucky, were shot and killed Thursday during a robbery, and a person of interest has been taken into custody, state police announced Friday. The suspect, described as approximately 6-foot-3 and wearing a light-colored hoodie, mask and gloves, fled the scene on foot after the shooting.

Kentucky State Police, the FBI, the Bureau of Alcohol Tobacco Firearms and Explosives, local police, and county sheriff's offices all participated in a multi-agency manhunt involving helicopters, drones, and dogs. Nearby schools were briefly placed on lockdown. It remains unclear whether the suspect left the bank with any money.

"This is a terrible day for Berea," state police spokesperson Trooper Scottie Pennington said at a news briefing. "They're our people that work in our community, and they're no longer with us." U.S. Bank expressed deep condolences and said it was cooperating fully with law enforcement and supporting the victims' families.

The identities of the two victims, a man and a woman, have not yet been released. The person of interest is described as believed to be involved in the robbery, though formal charges had not been publicly announced as of Friday morning.

stories.headline: Six Injured in Stabbing at Tacoma, Washington High School; Suspect Detained
stories.summary:
Six people were injured Thursday afternoon in a stabbing at Foss High School in Tacoma, Washington, after what police described as "some type of altercation." Five students and one adult security guard were transported to hospital by the Tacoma Fire Department; all were reported in stable condition as of Thursday afternoon.

The suspect, identified as a student at the school, was also injured and was taken into custody at the scene. He was booked on five counts of first-degree assault. Police said officers responded quickly and secured the scene promptly after receiving reports of an active assault at approximately 1:35 p.m. local time.

Foss High School will remain closed Friday and reopen Monday, May 4, with counselors on site to support students and staff. Authorities urged anyone with video footage of the incident to contact the Tacoma Police Department as the investigation continued into Thursday night.

The school has a history of violence; in 2007, a student was shot and killed in a hallway there. Thursday's mass stabbing adds to a series of school violence incidents that have drawn national attention in recent years.

stories.headline: Chinese Court Rules AI-Driven Layoff Unlawful in Landmark Labor Case
stories.summary:
An appeals court in Hangzhou, China, has ruled that a tech company's dismissal of a worker whose job was replaced by artificial intelligence was unlawful, in what legal observers are describing as a significant early precedent for AI-related labor disputes. The worker was let go after the company determined that AI tools could perform his functions, a scenario playing out with increasing frequency across technology sectors worldwide.

The court's ruling does not prohibit companies from adopting AI, but found that the specific manner of this dismissal violated Chinese labor law protections. The case has drawn attention in China's legal and business communities as employers race to integrate AI systems while workers and regulators struggle to define the boundaries of permissible automation-driven restructuring.

The decision arrives as governments and courts in multiple countries grapple with how existing labor frameworks apply to AI displacement. China, which has one of the world's largest technology workforces, has yet to enact comprehensive legislation specifically governing AI-related layoffs, making court rulings like this one influential in shaping employer behavior in the interim.

Labor advocates say the case highlights the urgent need for updated worker protections as AI adoption accelerates, while business groups warn that overly restrictive interpretations could slow productivity gains and discourage investment in automation technologies.

stories.headline: NYC Mayoral Candidate Mamdani Proposes Pied-à-Terre Tax, Citing Ken Griffin's $238 Million Penthouse
stories.summary:
New York City mayoral candidate Zohran Mamdani announced a proposed pied-à-terre tax on non-primary luxury residences, citing hedge fund billionaire Ken Griffin's $238 million Midtown penthouse as a prime example of the wealth the levy would target. The press release naming Griffin sparked a sharp backlash from some of New York's wealthiest residents, who rallied to the Citadel founder's defense.

Mamdani, running on a progressive platform, framed the tax as a way to generate city revenue from ultra-luxury properties owned by part-time residents while middle-class New Yorkers face housing costs. Supporters argue that taxing vacant or seldom-used high-end properties could ease budget pressures and fund public services without burdening working residents.

Critics, including wealthy donors and business groups, contend that singling out individuals by name in policy announcements is politically motivated and could deter high-net-worth individuals and investment from the city. Griffin himself has previously clashed with progressive politicians over tax and regulatory proposals in other jurisdictions.

The proposal adds to a crowded field of policy debates in New York City's mayoral race, where housing affordability and wealth inequality are central issues. Whether such a tax could clear the state legislature, which would likely need to authorize it, remains an open question.

stories.headline: American Farmers Increasingly Ignoring USDA Surveys as Confidence in Government Data Erodes
stories.summary:
American farmers are declining to participate in U.S. Department of Agriculture surveys at growing rates, the latest indicator of deepening distrust in federal data collection, according to a Bloomberg report published Friday. The trend threatens the reliability of agricultural statistics that commodity markets, policymakers, and food supply chains depend on.

The USDA relies heavily on voluntary farmer participation to produce its widely watched crop production, acreage, and inventory estimates. As response rates fall, the agency faces greater difficulty producing accurate assessments, a concern that has grown more acute following recent scrutiny of USDA methodology after significant discrepancies were found in corn acreage estimates.

The erosion of farmer trust in government institutions has been building for years and spans political affiliations, though it has intensified amid broader rural skepticism of federal agencies. Some farmers cite concerns about how their data is used, while others express general distrust of Washington policymaking on agricultural issues.

Inaccurate or incomplete USDA data can cause significant volatility in futures markets and complicate planning for food processors, exporters, and retailers. Agricultural economists warn that without credible, timely data, the U.S. risks undermining one of its most important tools for managing domestic and global food supply transparency.

stories.headline: Global Economy Faces Dual Pressure as Energy Shock and AI Transformation Collide
stories.summary:
The global economy is navigating two powerful and opposing forces simultaneously: an energy price shock driven by geopolitical conflict and supply disruptions, and a technology-driven productivity wave fueled by rapid artificial intelligence deployment, Bloomberg reports. Economists warn the combination creates unusually complex crosscurrents that defy straightforward policy responses.

Energy costs have surged amid ongoing conflict affecting major oil-producing regions, squeezing manufacturers, logistics companies, and consumers worldwide. At the same time, AI-driven automation is beginning to show measurable productivity gains in certain sectors, potentially offsetting some inflationary pressure but also accelerating workforce displacement in others.

Central banks and finance ministries are struggling to calibrate responses to an environment where traditional inflation and growth signals are being distorted by structural technological change layered on top of a commodity shock. The divergence between sectors benefiting from AI adoption and those hammered by energy costs is widening inequality across and within national economies.

Analysts say the interaction between these forces could produce sharply different outcomes depending on how quickly AI productivity gains diffuse through the broader economy and whether energy markets stabilize. The uncertainty is complicating investment decisions for businesses and long-term budget planning for governments globally.

stories.headline: Brazil's Congress Overrides Lula Veto, Dramatically Reduces Bolsonaro's 27-Year Sentence
stories.summary:
Brazil's Congress voted Thursday to override President Luiz Inácio Lula da Silva's veto of legislation that would sharply reduce former President Jair Bolsonaro's 27-year prison sentence to just over two years. More than two-thirds of the conservative-majority Congress backed the bill, which changes how prison sentences are calculated for individuals convicted of coup-related acts. Bolsonaro, 71, was sentenced last year for his role in attempting to remain in power after losing the 2022 election to Lula.

The override represents a significant political defeat for Lula and his left-wing government, coming just one day after the Senate rejected his nominee for a Supreme Court seat — the first such rejection in decades. Lawmakers loudly chanted the name of Bolsonaro's eldest son, Senator Flavio Bolsonaro, who called the vote a birthday present and has his own presidential ambitions.

Bolsonaro had been temporarily placed under house arrest in March due to health concerns. The Supreme Court found he was aware of plans to assassinate Lula and his vice president, though the conspiracy ultimately failed when senior military leaders withheld support.

The legislation could still face a challenge before Brazil's Supreme Court. The twin legislative defeats for Lula come as he campaigns for a fourth presidential term and as polls show the younger Bolsonaro running roughly even with him, underscoring the deeply polarized state of Brazilian politics.

stories.headline: U.S. Sanctions Former DR Congo President Kabila Over Alleged M23 Rebel Support
stories.summary:
The United States imposed sweeping sanctions Thursday on former Democratic Republic of Congo President Joseph Kabila, accusing him of providing financial support to the M23 rebel group, encouraging defections from the Congolese army, and seeking to launch attacks on the military from abroad. All of Kabila's U.S.-based assets have been frozen, and American citizens and companies are barred from conducting business with him.

Washington said the measures are part of a broader effort to support a regional peace deal brokered last year between DR Congo and Rwanda. The U.S. has previously sanctioned Rwandan army commanders over their alleged backing of M23, which Kigali denies despite what international observers describe as overwhelming evidence.

Kabila, 54, led DR Congo for 18 years before leaving power in 2019. He was sentenced to death in absentia by a Congolese military court last September on war crimes and treason charges, which he denied. His current whereabouts are unknown, though he was spotted in the M23-held city of Goma a year ago.

Eastern DR Congo has been engulfed in conflict for decades, with M23 making major territorial advances in early 2025 and seizing large portions of the mineral-rich region. The U.S. Treasury said the sanctions also support a critical minerals supply chain transparency agreement signed between Washington and Kinshasa in December.

stories.headline: Saudi Arabia's Sports Empire Under Strain as PIF Pulls Funding From LIV Golf
stories.summary:
Saudi Arabia's Public Investment Fund announced it will stop bankrolling the LIV Golf tour at the end of the current season, casting doubt over the future of the breakaway series and raising broader questions about the kingdom's vast sports investment strategy. The PIF had spent tens of billions of pounds over the past decade positioning Saudi Arabia as a global sports hub, hosting Formula 1, boxing, tennis, and acquiring a majority stake in Premier League club Newcastle United.

The withdrawal follows a major Saudi budget deficit of $73 billion last year, driven by increased government spending and lower oil revenues. Industry sources told the BBC that a strategic shift was underway inside the PIF late last year, with a new emphasis on sustainable investments that generate returns rather than prestige-driven spending. The postponement of the 2029 Asian Winter Games and the departure of the WTA Finals from Saudi Arabia were early indicators of the changing posture.

The PIF issued a statement insisting it remains committed to sports as a priority sector and will continue substantial international investments. Saudi Arabia is still scheduled to host the men's FIFA World Cup in 2034, and the kingdom continues to fund major domestic football with high-profile signings such as Cristiano Ronaldo.

Critics have long accused Saudi Arabia of using sports spending to 'sportswash' its human rights record, a charge that gained renewed attention after the 2018 killing of journalist Jamal Khashoggi. Whether the PIF retrenchment signals a genuine strategic pivot or a temporary recalibration amid fiscal pressure remains a key question for the global sports industry.

stories.headline: Corruption Scandals Erode Support for Argentina's President Milei
stories.summary:
A series of corruption scandals is testing the political durability of Argentine President Javier Milei, whose libertarian reform agenda had initially attracted broad popular support. Bloomberg Politics reports that the controversies are stretching the boundaries of voter tolerance for a leader who had built his brand around anti-establishment, anti-corruption politics.

Milei swept to power in late 2023 promising to slash government spending and dismantle what he called Argentina's corrupt political class. His economic shock therapy, including a sharp devaluation of the peso and deep public spending cuts, produced painful short-term effects but began to bring down Argentina's historically extreme inflation rate.

The emerging scandals have given political opponents fresh ammunition and complicated Milei's efforts to consolidate power ahead of midterm elections. His government has not yet detailed the specific nature of all allegations, and supporters argue that opposition forces are weaponizing any misstep to undermine a reform program that threatens entrenched interests.

Argentina's political trajectory is being closely watched by international investors and by the IMF, with which the country has a major financing arrangement. Whether the scandals meaningfully damage Milei's standing or prove a passing difficulty will depend in part on whether his economic program continues to show measurable results for ordinary Argentines.

stories.headline: Oscar for Anti-Putin Documentary Goes Missing After Lufthansa Checked-Bag Incident at JFK
stories.summary:
Russian exile and filmmaker Pavel Talankin arrived in Germany to find his Academy Award statuette missing after airport security at John F. Kennedy International Airport prevented him from carrying it aboard his Lufthansa flight as hand luggage. TSA officers told Talankin the golden award could be used as a weapon, and Lufthansa staff helped box it for the hold — but when he landed, the Oscar was gone.

Talankin co-directed Mr Nobody Against Putin, the Best Feature Documentary winner at this year's Academy Awards. The film documents the spread of war propaganda in a Russian school following Russia's 2022 invasion of Ukraine. Talankin is now exiled from Russia, and Russian authorities have banned the documentary from streaming platforms on grounds that it "propagates extremism and terrorism."

Lufthansa said it is conducting a "comprehensive internal search" and expressed deep regret over the incident. The BBC said it had contacted the TSA and the Academy of Motion Picture Arts and Sciences. The executive producer, Robin Hessman, told the BBC that Talankin had previously traveled multiple times with the Oscar and his BAFTA without incident, and said: "This wouldn't have happened to Leonardo DiCaprio."

An Academy Award stands roughly 34 centimeters tall and weighs nearly four kilograms. The incident has drawn attention to both airport security practices and the broader vulnerability of foreign-language filmmakers navigating American bureaucracy.

stories.headline: Lindsey Vonn Says She Is Still in 'Survival Mode' Months After Near-Amputation Olympic Crash
stories.summary:
Alpine ski legend Lindsey Vonn said Thursday she remains in "survival mode" and has not yet decided whether she will retire from competitive skiing following her catastrophic crash at the 2026 Winter Olympics in Cortina, Italy. The 41-year-old has undergone eight operations after fracturing her tibia and breaking her ankle in the women's downhill event in February, an injury she has previously said nearly cost her the leg.

Vonn told the Associated Press she does not want to make a premature decision about her future. She said at least one more surgery remains — to remove hardware and replace her ACL — followed by roughly six months of recovery, meaning she faces a minimum of 18 months before she could return to full training.

"I may retire. I may never race again and that would be completely fine, but I'm not in a position emotionally to make that decision at this point," Vonn said. She described the pain from this injury as unlike anything she had experienced in a career marked by serious knee and leg injuries, saying the severity and the prospect of amputation placed it "not even in the universe" of her previous recoveries.

Vonn, who has won 84 World Cup races and is second on the all-time women's list behind Mikaela Shiffrin, had previously retired in 2019 before returning to competition in 2024 following a partial right knee replacement. She was considered a medal contender at what she had described as her final Olympics.

stories.headline: Senate Unanimously Bans Members and Staff From Betting on Prediction Markets
stories.summary:
The Senate passed a bipartisan resolution Thursday by unanimous voice vote prohibiting senators and their staff from participating in prediction markets, platforms where users wager on the outcomes of political, economic, and geopolitical events. The measure takes effect immediately as a change to Senate rules. It was sponsored by Sen. Bernie Moreno (R-Ohio) and broadened by an amendment from Sen. Alex Padilla (D-Calif.) to include staff.

The vote comes one week after a U.S. special forces soldier was charged with using classified information to bet on prediction markets, specifically on the January capture of Venezuelan then-president Nicolás Maduro. Lawmakers have also raised concerns about who may be profiting from wagers tied to the ongoing U.S.-Iran conflict. Earlier this month, the Associated Press reported that new accounts on the platform Polymarket made suspiciously well-timed bets on a U.S.-Iran ceasefire, generating hundreds of thousands of dollars in profit.

Senate Minority Leader Chuck Schumer called the ban a 'no-brainer,' saying Congress must never become 'a casino where members can gamble on wars or economic crises.' Sen. Todd Young (R-Ind.) called it 'a good first step' and urged the Senate to advance a broader bill he co-introduced that would extend the ban to all federal employees and elected officials.

Prediction markets including Polymarket and Kalshi have expanded rapidly and attracted growing regulatory scrutiny. Donald Trump Jr. serves as an adviser to both platforms, and the Trump administration has supported the industry in legal battles against states seeking to restrict it. Senate Minority Leader Schumer also called on the House and the White House to adopt similar restrictions.

stories.headline: Apparent Epstein Suicide Note Found by Cellmate Weeks Before His 2019 Death
stories.summary:
A cellmate of Jeffrey Epstein reportedly discovered an apparent suicide note tucked inside a graphic novel in the high-profile inmate's jail cell in late July 2019, weeks before Epstein was found dead on August 10, 2019. Nicholas Tartaglione, the cellmate, told the New York Times he found the note on a piece of yellow paper after Epstein had been found unresponsive with red marks on his neck — an episode that placed Epstein on suicide watch. The note reportedly read: 'What do you want me to do, bust out crying? Time to say goodbye.'

Tartaglione's lawyers made two unsuccessful attempts to authenticate the note before it was verified in late 2019 or early 2020. The note itself is now held in a vault at a New York courthouse. The New York Times filed a request Thursday to have the note unsealed. A DOJ spokesperson told the paper the agency had never had the note in its possession, and no copy appears in the publicly available Epstein files on the DOJ's website, though a two-page internal report references its existence.

Epstein's death was ruled a suicide by the New York City medical examiner, a conclusion the DOJ corroborated in July 2025. The case has remained a source of persistent public speculation, and a congressional watchdog recently agreed to open a second review of the DOJ's handling of Epstein file redactions.

The revelation adds new detail to the timeline surrounding Epstein's final weeks but does not alter the official ruling. The note's existence was known to Tartaglione's attorneys but had not previously been widely reported.

stories.headline: China Scraps Tariffs for 53 African Nations as Trade Rivalry With U.S. Intensifies
stories.summary:
China will eliminate tariffs for 53 African countries effective Friday, expanding a zero-tariff policy that previously applied only to 33 least-developed nations on the continent. The sole exception is Eswatini, which maintains diplomatic ties with Taiwan. The policy is set to remain in place through April 2028. Beijing has described itself as the first major economy to extend unilateral zero-tariff treatment to Africa.

The move is widely seen as a soft-power play amid the ongoing global trade disruption caused by U.S. tariff policies. Many African nations were hit with U.S. tariffs of up to 30% before courts struck down a portion of those duties; most now face a 10% U.S. tariff. Analysts say China is deliberately positioning itself as a trade-friendly alternative to Washington.

However, experts caution that the practical economic impact may be limited in the near term. Africa runs a growing trade deficit with China — last year that gap rose 65% to roughly $102 billion — and structural constraints including weak logistics, limited industrial capacity, and reliance on raw commodity exports mean tariff reductions alone are unlikely to rebalance trade. More industrialized economies such as South Africa and Morocco are expected to benefit most.

Longer-term, analysts say the policy could meaningfully expand trade if African countries are able to diversify exports and build out production capacity. Chinese consumer demand is also shifting in ways that could open new markets for African agricultural and manufactured goods.

stories.headline: Britney Spears Charged With DUI Following March Arrest in Southern California
stories.summary:
California prosecutors have filed a misdemeanor driving under the influence charge against pop star Britney Spears stemming from her arrest on March 4 on a Southern California highway. The Ventura County District Attorney's Office filed the single count — driving under the influence of any alcohol or drug — on Thursday. Spears, 44, was arrested after authorities alleged she was driving her BMW erratically at high speed.

Spears reportedly checked herself into a rehabilitation facility following the arrest. Her representatives described the incident as 'completely inexcusable' and said her sons and loved ones would work on a plan to support her well-being. Her arraignment is scheduled for next Monday, though as a misdemeanor charge she is not required to appear in person.

Prosecutors said the charge typically carries no jail time when no crash or injury occurred and the defendant has no prior similar offenses. Spears will likely be offered the option to plead guilty to a lesser charge of reckless driving, with a standard outcome of 12 months' probation, completion of a DUI class, and fines.

Spears is one of the best-selling music artists of all time. From 2008 to 2021 she was subject to a court-ordered conservatorship that controlled her finances and personal life before it was terminated following a prolonged public legal battle.

stories.headline: Update: Canada Pitches Sub-Arctic Port of Churchill as Gateway to Europe Amid U.S. Trade Tensions
stories.summary:
Canada is accelerating plans to expand the Port of Churchill, its only Arctic deep-water seaport, as Prime Minister Mark Carney pursues a goal of doubling non-U.S. exports within a decade. Located on Hudson Bay in northern Manitoba, the port offers a shorter shipping route to Europe, Africa, and South America compared to routes through southern Canadian ports. Ottawa has already invested C$320 million in the facility since 2018, when a consortium of Indigenous and community groups took ownership following years of mismanagement under a Denver-based private operator.

Carney has identified the Churchill expansion as a transformational project capable of shipping critical minerals, grain, liquified natural gas, and other goods to global markets. The port is currently operational only four to five months a year due to Arctic conditions, but climate change is expected to extend that window. Rail access through southern Manitoba connects the port to resource-rich western Canada.

The push reflects a broader Canadian strategy to reduce economic dependence on the United States in the face of escalating U.S. tariff pressure. Churchill's mayor and the Arctic Gateway Group, which now owns the port, see expansion as an opportunity to generate hundreds of jobs in a remote community of roughly 1,000 residents that has historically relied on seasonal tourism.

Analysts note that while geography favors Churchill, years of underinvestment and questions about commercial viability have historically stalled ambitions for the facility. The current political and trade climate has given the project renewed momentum at both the federal and local level.

stories.headline: House Passes DHS Funding Bill, Ending 76-Day Shutdown
stories.summary:
The House passed legislation Thursday to fund most Department of Homeland Security subagencies, ending a record-breaking 76-day shutdown of the department. Republican leadership used a procedural maneuver to bring the Senate spending bill directly to the floor, relying on Democratic cooperation to secure passage. The bill funds most DHS agencies but excludes Immigration and Customs Enforcement. President Trump signed the measure, ending weeks of financial hardship for federal workers left without pay.

The shutdown had caused widespread disruption at airports and across federal operations as intra-party Republican disputes over funding for Trump's immigration enforcement agenda stalled action for more than two months. Speaker Mike Johnson was ultimately forced to bypass opposition within his own conference to move the bill forward.

Supporters of the bill framed its passage as a necessary restoration of government function, while conservatives who had held out argued the exclusion of ICE funding represented a failure to fully back the administration's immigration priorities. Democrats who provided key votes said they acted to protect federal workers and restore agency operations, not to endorse the administration's broader immigration agenda.

The 76-day shutdown was the longest ever experienced by a single cabinet department. The resolution leaves open a separate fight over ICE appropriations, which is expected to resurface in upcoming spending negotiations.

stories.headline: Trump Threatens Troop Cuts in Germany, Italy, Spain as Feud with Merz Escalates
stories.summary:
President Trump announced Thursday that the United States is "studying and reviewing" whether to reduce the approximately 36,000 active-duty troops stationed in Germany, escalating a public dispute with German Chancellor Friedrich Merz over the conduct of the war with Iran. Trump said a decision would be made "over the next short period of time" and also suggested he would consider pulling troops from NATO allies Italy and Spain, saying Italy "has not been of any help" and Spain "has been horrible."

The confrontation was triggered earlier this week when Merz told university students that the U.S. had "no strategy" in Iran and that Tehran's negotiators had effectively humiliated Washington by allowing American officials to travel to Islamabad and leave without results. Trump responded by accusing Merz of wanting Iran to have nuclear weapons and saying Germany was "doing so poorly" economically and on immigration.

Merz visited German troops in northern Germany Thursday without directly addressing Trump's latest remarks, but went out of his way to emphasize the importance of NATO and "transatlantic solidarity." He separately told reporters his personal relationship with Trump "remains just as good as before," a characterization Trump's public statements appeared to contradict.

The exchange underscores deepening fractures in the transatlantic alliance over the Iran conflict. European capitals, particularly Berlin, Paris, and London, have grown increasingly skeptical of U.S. strategy, while the Trump administration has shown mounting impatience with allied criticism. Any reduction of U.S. forces in Germany would represent a dramatic shift in the postwar security architecture of Western Europe.

stories.headline: Update: Oil Spikes to $126 a Barrel on Report Trump to Be Briefed on New Iran Strike Plans
stories.summary:
Oil prices surged to their highest level since 2022 on Thursday after Axios reported that U.S. Central Command has prepared plans for a wave of "short and powerful" strikes on Iran intended to break the deadlock in ceasefire negotiations. Brent crude touched $126.31 per barrel before falling back sharply to around $114, partly because of the expiration of June futures contracts. The more actively traded July contract settled around $110 a barrel.

The proposed strike plans reportedly include infrastructure targets and a separate option for seizing part of the Strait of Hormuz to reopen it for commercial shipping, potentially involving ground troops. About 20 percent of the world's oil and liquefied natural gas normally transits the strait, which remains effectively closed since the start of the conflict, driving sustained upward pressure on global energy prices.

The price spike compounded already significant economic pain for consumers. In the United Kingdom, petrol prices have risen approximately 24 pence per liter compared to pre-war levels, and the government has warned of broader increases in food, energy, and airfare costs. Fertilizer prices have also begun rising, which analysts say could feed through to food prices in coming months.

The White House and U.S. Central Command had not confirmed the Axios report as of Thursday evening. Iran's Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei separately issued a defiant statement saying the Islamic Republic would protect its nuclear and missile capabilities and that the only place for Americans in the Persian Gulf was "at the bottom of its waters," signaling Tehran's continued resistance to a broader deal.

stories.headline: Update: Violence Erupts Outside Australian Hospital After Arrest in Murder of Five-Year-Old Girl
stories.summary:
A man suspected of murdering five-year-old Kumanjayi Little Baby was arrested late Thursday night in Alice Springs, Australia, after being spotted by community members and subjected to what police described as a "sustained attack" that left him unconscious. The girl's body had been found earlier Thursday, days after she was last seen being put to bed at an Aboriginal town camp near the city on Saturday night.

Following the arrest, roughly 200 people gathered outside the Alice Springs hospital where the suspect, identified as Jefferson Lewis, was being treated. Crowds threw projectiles at police and attempted to set fire to at least one police vehicle. Officers deployed tear gas to disperse the crowd, and several people, including emergency service workers, were injured. At least one person was arrested.

Northern Territory Police Commissioner Martin Dole said Lewis's injuries were not serious enough to require continued hospitalization and he was flown to Darwin and transferred to police custody. Charges are expected within days. Dole also warned that investigators believe someone in the community helped Lewis evade capture, saying "we're coming for you as well."

The girl's grandfather called for calm on Friday. An Alyawarre community leader cautioned that invoking the concept of traditional "payback" in connection with the case risked inflaming further violence, and said the Western legal system was the appropriate avenue for justice. Forensic tests to confirm the child's identity and cause of death were continuing Friday.

stories.headline: Venice Biennale Jury Resigns Over Russia's Return, Nine Days Before Opening
stories.summary:
The entire jury of the Venice Biennale resigned Thursday, just nine days before the prestigious art exhibition's inauguration, citing the return of Russia to the event for the first time since its full-scale invasion of Ukraine. The jury said it could not consider for prizes any country "whose leaders are currently charged with crimes against humanity," a standard it applied to both Russia and Israel, given outstanding International Criminal Court warrants against their respective leaders.

The controversy has intensified over recent weeks. The European Union withdrew a two-million-euro grant over Russia's participation, calling it "morally wrong" given Moscow's ongoing efforts to destroy Ukrainian cultural heritage. Italy's culture ministry dispatched a delegation to Venice this week to gather information, and the minister himself declared a personal boycott of the event. Prime Minister Giorgia Meloni said Russia's inclusion was "a decision not shared by the government" but acknowledged the Biennale's institutional autonomy.

The Biennale's leadership has maintained that it is open to all and "rejects any form of exclusion or censorship," arguing it has no legal basis to ban Russia since the country owns its pavilion outright. Russia's team is scheduled to exhibit a sound performance called "The Tree is Rooted in the Sky," though some reports indicate public access may be restricted.

Israel's foreign ministry previously called the jury's decision to exclude Israel from prize consideration "a contamination of the art world." The resignations leave the exhibition without a functioning jury days before its opening, raising questions about whether awards will be given at all this year. For Russia, a return to the Biennale would represent a significant step toward international cultural rehabilitation while its invasion of Ukraine continues.

stories.headline: Myanmar's Aung San Suu Kyi Moved to House Arrest After Five Years in Military Detention
stories.summary:
Myanmar's military junta announced Thursday that former leader Aung San Suu Kyi, 80, has been moved from what is believed to have been a military prison to house arrest, in what analysts described as part of a public relations offensive by the regime. State media broadcast a photograph of the Nobel laureate sitting with two uniformed personnel, though the image was taken in 2022. Military chief Min Aung Hlaing, who led the 2021 coup that ousted her government, said he had "commuted her remaining sentence to be served at the designated residence."

Suu Kyi's son Kim Aris said he remained skeptical of the announcement, noting he had no proof she was alive and had received no communication from her in years. Her legal team told Reuters they had received no direct official notification of the transfer. "Until I'm allowed communication with her, or somebody can independently verify her condition and her whereabouts, then I won't believe anything," Aris said.

Suu Kyi was arrested on the day of the coup and subsequently tried on charges widely dismissed as fabricated, accumulating a 33-year sentence that has been reduced several times. She has not been heard from publicly since her coup-era arrest, and her lawyers have not seen her in more than three years.

Observers said the announcement likely reflects the junta's desire to improve its international standing. The military recently held elections restoring a nominally democratic government while leaving the same leadership in place, and has recorded battlefield gains against armed opposition groups. Former Suu Kyi economic adviser Sean Turnell told the BBC the regime was "trying to convince the world that it's a legitimate government" and that the reported transfer was "part and parcel of that" effort.
